summary: DHH argues that version numbers in open source projects like Rails carry far more psychological weight than they deserve. He explains that a release is simply an alias for a particular code revision, not a magical quality milestone. The post criticizes clients and developers who refuse to use newer unreleased revisions even when they contain fixes for problems they're actively experiencing. He encourages developers to take control of their own release schedules by pinning to specific revisions rather than waiting for official releases, noting that edge is typically higher quality than the last release due to accumulated bug fixes.
key_points: Version numbers in open source are cultural markers, not meaningful quality indicators — a release is just an alias for a specific code revision
People irrationally avoid newer unreleased code even when it fixes bugs they're actively suffering from
High-level frameworks like Rails don't carry the same instability risks as low-level systems like databases or operating systems
Open source gives developers the power to control their own release schedule by pinning to specific revisions
Edge/trunk code is typically higher quality than the last official release because it contains accumulated bug fixes
money_quotes: Nothing magical happens when a certain revision of the code base is blessed with a release. It's simply the decision that what we have now should have a label.
Slapping 2.0 on something is a license to break existing code that has been deprecated in the past.
Versions are meaningless, just that they're more about culture and information than about the quality of software.
If you're encountering a bug or desiring a feature that's been included in the latest edge version, you're not doing yourself any favors by waiting for the whim of a release.
The great thing about open source is that you can control your own release schedule.
People do not differentiate between low-level systems, like their OS, web server, or database server, and high-level frameworks like Rails.
Most of the time, edge is of considerably higher quality than the last released version because we've been committing loads of bug fixes since then.
key_insight: Version numbers are cultural artifacts, not quality guarantees, and developers should pin to specific revisions that solve their problems rather than waiting for the ceremony of an official release.
